revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Hiro Hachiman Shrine]][[fr:Hiro Hachiman-jinja]][[ja:広八幡神社]]
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Hirogawa, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name =Hiro Hachiman Jinja | native_name = 広八幡神社 | image = File:Hiro hachiman.jpg | caption = Hiro Hachiman Jinja | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.01769444|N|135.17527778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Hiro Hachiman Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = c. {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}} | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 206 Kaminakano Hirogawa-cho Arida-gun Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official website|1=http://hirohachimanjinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = October 1 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} | location = {{ill|Hirogawa|qid=Q1345650|1=Hirogawa, Wakayama}} }} {{nihongo|'''Hiro Hachiman Jinja'''|広八幡神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the town of {{ill|Hirogawa, Wakayama|en|Hirogawa, Wakayama|qid=Q1345650|lt=Hirogawa|1=Hirogawa, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine is dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. It contains a number of structures which are designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. The shrine is also known for its monument to local hero {{ill|Hamaguchi Goryō|en|Hamaguchi Goryō|qid=Q5586998|lt=Hamaguchi Goryō|1=Hamaguchi Goryō}}, who saved local villagers from a {{ill|Tsunami|en|Tsunami|qid=Q8070|lt=Tsunami|1=Tsunami}} during the [[:en:1854 Ansei-Nankai earthquake|1854 Ansei-Nankai earthquake]], as recounted in “A Living God” by {{ill|Lafcadio Hearn|en|Lafcadio Hearn|qid=Q313638|lt=Lafcadio Hearn|1=Lafcadio Hearn}} in his ''Gleanings in Buddha Fields'' (1897). This monument with an inscription by [[:en:Katsu Kaishu|Katsu Kaishu]] and calligraphy [[Iwaya Ichiroku]], was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 2015.<ref name="Bunka0">{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/276576 |title=濱口梧陵碑 |trans-title=Hamaguchi goryō-hi|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==History== The history of this shrine is not well documented. Per the ''Kii-Zoku-Fudok'', one of the [[fudoki|ancient records]] of {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|qid=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}, the shrine was originally built in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|qid=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}} (reigned 539 to 571 AD) and relocated to this location in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. The main shrine is dedicated to the Hachiman triad of ''Hondawake-no-Mikoto'' ([[:en:Emperor Ojin|Emperor Ojin]]), ''Tarashinakatsuhiko-no-Mikoto'' ([[:en:Emperor Chuai|Emperor Chuai]]) and ''Okinagatarashihime-no-Mikoto'' (Emperor Jingu). Three subsidiary shrines are on the grounds, These are the Wakamiya-sha dedicated to ''Ohsagi-no-Mikoto'' (Emperor Nintoku), Kora-sha dedicated to [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] and Tenjin-sha dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|qid=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}.
The shrine was destroyed during {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s conquest of Kii Province in 1585, and was rebuilt by [[:en:Asano Yukinaga|Asano Yukinaga]] after he was assigned he province by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}. During the rule of the [[Lii Tokugawa family|Kii Tokugawa clan]], the shrine was supported by {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|qid=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain|1=Kishū Domain}} during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. With the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the [[:en:Shinbutsu Bunri|Separation of Shintoism and Buddhism]], the various Buddhist-related structures on the grounds of the shrine were destroyed or relocated. These included a Kannon-dō, [[Shōrō]] and [[Tahōtō]] (which was sent to {{ill|Mitaki-dera|en|Mitaki-dera|qid=Q63712|lt=Mitaki-dera|1=Mitaki-dera}} in {{ill|Hiroshima|en|Hiroshima|qid=Q34664|lt=Hiroshima|1=Hiroshima}}). The shrine retained its ''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|qid=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}}'' (two storied gate).
The shrine's ''{{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|qid=Q3022455|lt=Dengaku|1=Dengaku}}'' and {{ill|Lion dance|en|Lion dance|qid=Q713866|lt=Lion dance|1=Lion dance}} performances during its autumn festival has been designated Wakayama Prefecture Intangible Cultural Properties.
==Cultural Properties== * [[Honden]], built in 1413, repaired in 1776. National Important Cultural Property (ICP) from 1929<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2899|title=木広八幡神社本殿 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> *[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]], built in 1704, ICP from 1947<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2903|title=広八幡神社拝殿 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> *{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|qid=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}}, built in 1475, ICP from 1947<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2904|title=広八幡神社楼門 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> *Kora shrine Honden, built in 1502, ICP from 1929<ref name="Bunka4">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2901|title=広八幡神社摂社高良社本殿 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> *Tenjin-sha Honden, built in 1652, ICP from 1947<ref name="Bunka5">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2902|title=広八幡神社摂社天神社本殿 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> *Wakamiya Shrine Honden, built in 1493, ICP from 1929<ref name="Bunka6">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2900|title=広八幡神社摂社若宮社本殿 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Wakayama)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Wakayama)|qid=Q6573290|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Wakayama)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Wakayama)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[http://hirohachimanjinja.or.jp/english Official home page] *[https://www.town.hirogawa.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/rekishibunka/hirohachiman.html Hirogawa Town official home page]
<!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including--> {{Authority control}}
